text: When working with APIs, you typically send HTTP requests to a server and receive data in JSON format. For example, a GET request might return user information from a database, while a POST request could create a new record. Many APIs require authentication, such as an API key, to access their services. You also need to be aware of rate limits, which restrict how many requests you can make in a given time period. Error handling is essential; always check for status codes like 200 for success or 404 for not found. By understanding these concepts, you can use APIs effectively and build powerful applications.
